_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Union County, SC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses.
display_name: Union County, South Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Early log and frame courthouse. Union County was formed from the old Ninety Six District.
Brick courthouse. Survived the Civil War and Reconstruction without destruction. Served nearly ninety years before replacement.

record_losses.cause: Minor water damage and deterioration over time — no catastrophic event
record_losses.description: Union County's courthouse survived the Civil War and Reconstruction without major damage. No catastrophic fire has occurred. Some early records show age-related deterioration, and loose papers from the eighteenth century are fragmentary. The Ninety Six District records cover the pre-1785 period. Union County's records are generally well-preserved for the Upcountry.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Union County after 1785, the records are likely intact. The county's Reconstruction-era violence — KKK activity, political murders — generated federal court records that supplement the county records with testimony and detail the county records never captured.
record_losses.source: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.

deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Ninety Six District records cover pre-1785.
deeds.repository: Union County Register of Deeds; SC Department of Archives and History microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com SC land records

wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose papers are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Union County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds from formation. Licenses required statewide 1911.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1911 coverage through bonds and church records.
marriages.repository: Union County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.repository: SC Department of Archives and History; Union County Clerk of Court

vital_records.repository: SC DHEC; SC Department of Archives and History

substitute_sources.description: Union County was part of the old Ninety Six District before 1785. District records cover the pre-county period for the Broad River area.
substitute_sources.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for pre-1785 families in the Union County area.
substitute_sources.source: SC Department of Archives and History.

substitute_sources.source_type: Federal Ku Klux Klan trial records
substitute_sources.description: Union County experienced significant KKK violence during Reconstruction. Federal trial records and the Joint Select Committee hearings (1871–1872) include testimony from Union County victims and perpetrators.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives and Records Administration; published in KKK Testimony (13 vols., 1872)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for documenting both Black victims and white perpetrators. Testimony names individuals and describes events in detail not available from county records.
substitute_sources.source: Joint Select Committee to Inquire into the Condition of Affairs in the Late Insurrectionary States. Testimony. 13 vols. Washington, DC, 1872.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Union County enumerated from the first federal census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) are useful for understanding the cotton economy's decline.

narrative_hooks: Union County's records run unbroken from 1785, but the deepest testimony about Reconstruction-era life comes from the federal KKK trial records — sworn depositions that name names and describe violence the county records passed over in silence.
Before 1785, Union County was part of the Ninety Six District, and {ancestor_name}'s earliest records are under that jurisdiction at the SC Archives.

If {ancestor_name} lost land to the federal government for the Sumter National Forest in the 1930s, the condemnation records document the sale — and the price paid for three generations of family ground.

South Carolina's lack of marriage licenses before 1911 and vital records before 1915 means that for {ancestor_name}, church records and family Bibles are the primary evidence of births, marriages, and deaths.
source_references: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.
Joint Select Committee to Inquire into the Condition of Affairs in the Late Insurrectionary States. Testimony. 13 vols. Washington, DC, 1872.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: South Carolina County Records. Columbia, 1937–1942.
